import LegalLean.Defeasible

namespace LegalLean.CaseStudies.IRC163h

prompt.system:
You are a legal research assistant specialising in formal methods
applied to law. Your answers must be structured to support formalisation in Lean 4
dependent type theory. You are helping build a library called LegalLean that encodes
legal reasoning with machine-checkable guarantees.

CRITICAL: Structure your response as JSON with the following schema. Do NOT return
prose — return structured data that maps to type definitions.

Be exhaustive about conditions, exceptions, and vagueness. Legal formalisation
requires knowing EVERY rule, exception, and point of interpretive discretion.
prompt.user:
Analyse the following legal provision and return a JSON object
with this exact structure:

{
  "provision": {
    "id": "string — official citation",
    "title": "string — short name",
    "jurisdiction": "string",
    "source_url": "string — authoritative source URL"
  },
  "rules": [
    {
      "id": "string — unique rule identifier",
      "description": "string — what this rule establishes",
      "modality": "permitted | prohibited | obligatory",
      "conditions": [
        {
          "id": "string",
          "description": "string — natural language condition",
          "formalisable": true/false,
          "if_not_formalisable": "string — why (vague term? discretionary?)"
        }
      ],
      "exceptions": [
        {
          "id": "string",
          "description": "string — what defeats this rule",
          "priority": "string — why this exception wins (lex specialis, lex posterior, etc.)",
          "source": "string — statutory reference for the exception"
        }
      ],
      "definitions": [
        {
          "term": "string — defined term",
          "definition": "string — statutory definition",
          "has_open_texture": true/false,
          "core_meaning": "string — settled, uncontested meaning",
          "penumbra": "string — contested, unclear boundary cases"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "defeat_relations": [
    {
      "defeater_rule": "string — rule ID that defeats",
      "defeated_rule": "string — rule ID that is defeated",
      "reason": "string — why (lex specialis, lex posterior, lex superior, etc.)",
      "strict": true/false
    }
  ],
  "discretionary_checkpoints": [
    {
      "id": "string",
      "authority": "string — who decides",
      "decision": "string — what they decide",
      "constraints": ["string — limits on their discretion"],
      "gated_rule": "string — which rule this gates"
    }
  ],
  "open_texture_terms": [
    {
      "term": "string",
      "type": "vague | discretionary",
      "core": "string — clear cases",
      "penumbra": "string — unclear cases",
      "authority": "string — who resolves ambiguity",
      "case_law": ["string — key cases interpreting this term"]
    }
  ],
  "key_references": [
    {
      "author": "string",
      "title": "string",
      "year": "number",
      "relevance": "string",
      "url": "string"
    }
  ]
}

Legal provision to analyse:
IRC §163(h) Mortgage Interest Deduction

Additional context:
This is for the LegalLean monograph. We need to formalise this provision in Lean 4 dependent type theory.

The stub is in LegalLean/CaseStudies/IRC163h.lean at line 19.
Target LegalLean types: DefeasiblyHolds.

Code context:
  4. Demonstrates defeasibility (exceptions to the deduction)

  Reference: Lawsky, "A Logic for Statutes", Florida Tax Review (2017).
-/

import LegalLean.Core
import LegalLean.Defeasible

namespace LegalLean.CaseStudies.IRC163h

-- TODO: Formalise IRC §163(h) mortgage interest deduction
-- This is the first case study to implement.
-- Scope: qualified residence interest, acquisition indebtedness limits,
-- home equity indebtedness, and the TCJA modifications.


Be especially thorough about:
1. Every condition and sub-condition for the rule to apply
2. Every exception that can defeat the rule
3. Every term with open texture (vagueness or discretion)
4. The defeat priority ordering between rules
5. Who has discretion and what constrains it

rules.description: General prohibition on deduction of personal interest

conditions.description: Interest paid or accrued during the taxable year is personal interest
conditions.if_not_formalisable: Definition of 'personal interest' has exclusions that require interpretation (e.g., what constitutes 'investment interest')

exceptions.priority: statutory exception (expressly carved out from personal interest definition)

exceptions.description: Investment interest is deductible (subject to §163(d) limitations)

rules.description: Deduction allowed for qualified residence interest

conditions.description: Interest is paid or accrued on indebtedness with respect to qualified residence
conditions.if_not_formalisable: 'qualified residence' has open texture (main home vs. second home)

conditions.description: For acquisition indebtedness: secured by qualified residence AND proceeds used to acquire, construct, or substantial improvement
Acquisition indebtedness ≤ $750,000 ($375,000 MFS) after TCJA; $1M ($500k MFS) for indebtedness before 12/16/2017
For home equity indebtedness: secured by qualified residence AND ≤ $100,000 ($50,000 MFS)

exceptions.description: Interest on home equity loans suspended 2018-2025 (TCJA)

definitions.core_meaning: Principal residence + one other residence used personally during the year
definitions.penumbra: Time requirements for 'use', what constitutes 'use', RV/boat eligibility

definitions.definition: Indebtedness secured by qualified residence, proceeds used to acquire/construct/substantially improve (IRC §163(h)(3))

conditions.description: Taxpayer elects to treat refinanced debt as acquisition indebtedness

defeat_relations.reason: statutory exception (personal interest expressly excludes qualified residence interest)
lex posterior (TCJA §11045 suspended home equity interest deduction)

constraints: Must increase basis or be capital improvement

discretionary_checkpoints.decision: Treat refinanced acquisition indebtedness as qualifying

open_texture_terms.core: Principal residence + one clearly identified second home used >14 days or 10% of rental days
open_texture_terms.penumbra: Mobile homes, boats, time-sharing, fractional ownership, temporary absence
Repairs vs. improvements, cosmetic vs. structural

open_texture_terms.core: Recorded mortgage with qualified residence as sole security

key_references.author: Sarah B. Lawsky
key_references.title: A Logic for Statutes
key_references.relevance: Formal logic framework for defeasible statutory reasoning (LegalLean inspiration)

key_references.author: Treasury
key_references.title: Temp. Reg. §1.163-10T
key_references.relevance: Authoritative rules on qualified residence interest tracing and secured debt

consensus.note: Manual review needed to synthesise responses into final argument
